name: Bloomsdale Longstanding
description: The standard heirloom savoy-leaf spinach with deeply crinkled, glossy dark green leaves. Richer flavor than smooth-leaf types and more resistant to bolting. The 'Longstanding' name indicates its improved bolt resistance.
history: Introduced before 1826 and continuously grown ever since, making it one of the oldest heirloom vegetables still widely grown in America. Bloomsdale is a farm in Bristol, Pennsylvania where the variety was selected.

good.reason: Radishes mark rows, mature at different time

seasonal_quirks: Must be grown in cool weather — bolts quickly when days exceed 14 hours or temperatures rise above 75°F. Spring plantings should be timed to mature before heat. Excellent fall crop. Tolerates hard frost.
harvest_cues: Outer leaves can be harvested at any size. Full harvest when plant has 6–8 leaves and outer leaves are 4–6 inches. Cut outer leaves and plant continues to produce.
variety_notes: The savoyed (crinkled) leaf traps more grit than smooth spinach — wash thoroughly. The crinkled texture holds dressings and sauces better than flat types.
preservation: Blanch and freeze well. Wilt and freeze for cooking applications.
